description: Mater Amabilis Year 4 living books curriculum for grade 4. First encounters with Plutarch's Lives and Shakespeare; broader world history spanning the 17th–18th centuries; richer, more analytical narration.

books.title: George Washington's World
books.author: Genevieve Foster
books.subject: history
books.notes: Foster sets Washington's life beside what was happening across the whole world at the same time. Great for building a sense of simultaneous history. 'What was happening in another country the same year Washington was a boy?'

books.title: Trial and Triumph: Stories from Church History
books.author: Richard Hannula
books.subject: saints
books.notes: Short, vivid chapters on real believers who held to their faith under real cost. Ask: 'What gave this person the courage to keep going?'

books.title: Plutarch's Lives (selected passages, continuing rotation)
books.author: Plutarch
books.subject: history
books.notes: Continues the Plutarch rotation begun in Year 3, in the original archaic North translation. Read slowly, one incident at a time. 'Was this leader's choice wise, or only brave?'

books.title: Age of Fable (Bulfinch's Mythology)
books.author: Thomas Bulfinch
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: The Greek and Roman myths that shaped centuries of Western literature and art. Not history — story. 'Why do you think people told this story to explain the world?'

books.title: Robinson Crusoe
books.author: Daniel Defoe
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: One man's careful, practical rebuilding of a whole life from nothing. Good for discussing patience and providence. 'What would you have done differently on the island?'

books.title: Tales from Shakespeare: The Merchant of Venice (Lamb's prose retelling)
books.author: Charles and Mary Lamb
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: First taste of Shakespeare, in the Lambs' gentle prose retelling before the full play comes later in the Mater Amabilis rotation. 'Was Portia's solution fair to everyone?'

books.title: Madam How and Lady Why (first half)
books.author: Charles Kingsley
books.subject: science
books.notes: Kingsley explains earth science as a conversation with a curious child. Read a chapter, then go find a real example outdoors if possible. 'What question would you ask Madam How?'

books.title: The Storybook of Science
books.author: Jean-Henri Fabre
books.subject: science
books.notes: Fabre's grandfatherly narrator answers a child's endless nature questions. Excellent read-aloud pacing for this age. 'What did Uncle Paul notice that most people would miss?'

books.title: Gregor Mendel: The Friar Who Grew Peas
books.author: Cheryl Bardoe
books.subject: science
books.notes: A monk-scientist whose quiet, patient counting of pea plants in his monastery garden founded modern genetics. 'Why did it take so many years of counting before Mendel understood the pattern?'

books.title: At the Back of the North Wind
books.author: George MacDonald
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: A strange, tender story that takes a child's questions about suffering and death seriously rather than brushing past them. 'What did Diamond come to understand about North Wind that frightened him at first?'

books.title: Handbook of Nature Study (selections)
books.author: Anna Botsford Comstock
books.subject: nature_study
books.notes: A reference, not a read-aloud — dip in for whatever the child has just found outside. Pair every entry with a nature journal sketch. 'Draw what you saw, then look up one thing you didn't know about it.'

subject_plans.mathematics: Grade 4 scope (Common Core domains 4.OA, 4.NBT, 4.NF, 4.MD, 4.G): multi-digit multiplication and division with understanding of the standard algorithm's reasoning, not just its steps; fraction equivalence and comparison; adding and subtracting fractions with like denominators; measurement problems involving area and perimeter; classifying two-dimensional shapes by angles and line properties. Mater Amabilis approach: pose real problems the child must reason through aloud before showing any procedure — 'why does this work?' matters more than speed.
subject_plans.art_music: Composer: Johann Sebastian Bach — structured, mathematical, foundational Baroque music that rewards close listening (a Brandenburg Concerto, a fugue, a chorale). Discuss the patterns the child can hear repeating. Artist: Rembrandt van Rijn — dramatic light and shadow, deeply observed portraits and biblical scenes. Ask what the light draws the eye toward first. Poet: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow — narrative poems with strong rhythm and historical imagination (Paul Revere's Ride fits naturally alongside this year's American history), good for a child ready for longer, story-driven verse.
subject_plans.language_arts: Grade 4: studied dictation begins this year — the child studies a passage first, then writes it from memory, building spelling and punctuation instinct rather than rote copying. Formal grammar starts (parts of speech, sentence structure) taught through real sentences from that term's living books, not isolated drill. Narration shifts from purely oral toward including some written narration.
subject_plans.morning_time: A longer Scripture passage with brief discussion of its meaning, a hymn and folk song from that term's rotation, and Longfellow for poetry. This is also a good place to touch on the term's history timeline (George Washington's World) in a few connecting sentences.
